exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this Urban Studies assignment—my city data table. I picked a case study city but I’m not sure what counts as “good” sources or how detailed the race/ethnicity and GDP/income fields should be. Can you just tell me exactly what to put in each category (and what to do if my city doesn’t report something)?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill in “exactly what to put” for your specific city, but I can help you choose defensible sources and the right level of detail.

1) What city did you pick (and country)? And do you have to match a specific year (e.g., latest available or 2020/2022)?

2) For “good sources,” which of these do you think are most credible for your city, and why?
- National census bureau / national statistics office (population; race/ethnicity; income)
- City/regional planning department or open-data portal (area; sometimes population)
- International databases: World Bank, OECD, UNData, UN-Habitat, IMF (GDP/income proxies)
- Reputable compiled datasets: CIA World Factbook (country-level only), Brookings/academic reports (sometimes metro GDP)

3) Race/ethnicity detail: a practical rule is “use the finest breakdown that is (a) officially reported and (b) not absurdly long.”
- If your census reports 5–8 major categories, use those.
- If it reports dozens, aggregate into the top ~5–8 plus “Other/Multiple/Unknown.”
Question: does your country’s census report race, ethnicity, nationality, language, or something else? (Many countries *don’t* use ‘race’ as a category.)

4) GDP vs income: these are often reported at different geographic scales.
- GDP is often at national or metro/region level, not “city proper.”
- Income per capita might be median household income, mean income, or GNI per capita; definitions vary.
Question: are you studying the city proper, the metro area, or the broader region? (Your choice affects what’s acceptable.)

5) If the city doesn’t report something, don’t guess—document a fallback.
- Use a higher-level geography (metro/region/national) and label it clearly.
- Use a proxy (e.g., regional GDP per capita instead of city GDP) and state it.
- Put “Not reported/Not available” with a note explaining what you tried and why it’s missing.

If you tell me your city + whether you’re using city proper or metro, I’ll ask you a few quick questions to pin down: (a) the best 1–2 sources per field and (b) how many categories to use for race/ethnicity so your table looks complete and consistent.

exchanges.student:
I’m trying a different angle because I still feel stuck: can you give me a generic “template answer” for the table that I can basically copy/paste (like the exact fields + what units + a sample citation format), even if it’s not my specific city?

Also I haven’t locked a city yet—does it matter if I pick a city where race/ethnicity isn’t reported? That feels like it’ll break the assignment.
exchanges.tutor:
You can use a copy/paste *blank template* (with units + citation format) — that’s fair. You’ll still need to fill it with your city’s numbers.

**Geographic & Demographic Data Table (Template)**
| Category | Value | Year | Geography (city/metro/region) | Unit/Definition | Source (citation) | Notes/Assumptions |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Country / Admin level |  |  |  | e.g., municipality, city proper | Author/Agency (Year) |  |
| Latitude/Longitude (city center) |  |  |  | decimal degrees | Author/Agency (Year) |  |
| Total population |  |  |  | persons | Author/Agency (Year) |  |
| Population growth rate (optional) |  |  |  | % per year | Author/Agency (Year) |  |
| Race/Ethnicity (or closest official demographic breakdown) |  |  |  | % of population; list categories | Author/Agency (Year) | define categories exactly as source uses |
| Physical area |  |  |  | km² (or mi²) | Author/Agency (Year) | specify land vs total area |
| Population density (optional) |  |  |  | people/km² | Calculated from above | show calculation |
| Income per capita (or median income) |  |  |  | currency/person/year OR median household | Author/Agency (Year) | note income type + PPP/nominal |
| GDP (city/metro/region) |  |  |  | currency/year; nominal or PPP | Author/Agency (Year) | clarify geography if not city proper |
| Köppen climate zone |  |  |  | e.g., Cfa, BSh | Author/Agency (Year) | include map/data method |
| Data add-on (your choice) |  |  |  | e.g., elevation; coastal?; % informal housing | Author/Agency (Year) | why relevant |

**Sample citation formats (pick one style and be consistent):**
- **APA-ish (web):** Agency Name. (Year). *Title of dataset/table*. URL (Accessed 2026-03-10)
- **APA-ish (report):** Author/Agency. (Year). *Report Title* (p. xx). Publisher/URL
- **Dataset note in table:** “City Statistics Office (2023)” and then full reference in a references section.

Now your second question: **No, it won’t “break” the assignment if race/ethnicity isn’t reported.** The key is to use the *closest official demographic breakdown available* (often nationality, language, migrant status, religion, caste, etc.) and write a short note: “Race not collected in national census; used ethnicity/language as reported by ___.”

To make this manageable, decide this:
1) Do you want a city where an English-friendly census table exists (easier), or are you okay using international datasets + a few local PDFs?
2) Are you aiming for **city proper** data, or is **metro/region** acceptable for GDP?

Answer those two and I’ll help you pick a city that won’t cause data dead-ends.

exchanges.student: That didn’t work; trying a different angle: I’d rather not pick a city yet—can you just list 5 “easy-mode” cities where all these fields (including race/ethnicity + GDP + Köppen zone) are straightforward to find, and tell me which exact sources I should use for each city?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t pick everything for you end-to-end, but I *can* give you 5 “easy-mode” city options and the most straightforward source set to pull each field. Then you choose one and do the extraction.

### 5 easy-mode cities (data tends to be accessible + well-documented)
1) **New York City (USA)**
- Population + race/ethnicity + income: **U.S. Census Bureau (ACS via data.census.gov)**
- GDP (metro): **U.S. BEA — GDP by metropolitan area**
- Area: **NYC Open Data** or Census “Gazetteer”
- Köppen: **Beck et al. Köppen-Geiger dataset** (map/data)

2) **Los Angeles (USA)**
- Population + race/ethnicity + income: **U.S. Census Bureau (ACS)**
- GDP (metro): **U.S. BEA — metro GDP**
- Area: **Census Gazetteer** / city data portal
- Köppen: **Köppen-Geiger (Beck)**

3) **Toronto (Canada)**
- Population + “visible minority”/ethnic origin + income: **Statistics Canada (Census Profile)**
- GDP (metro): **Statistics Canada — GDP by CMA**
- Area: **City of Toronto Open Data** (or StatCan geographic profiles)
- Köppen: **Köppen-Geiger (Beck)**

4) **London (UK)**
- Population + ethnicity + income: **UK ONS** (Census; income often via ONS small-area/earnings datasets)
- GDP (region/metro-ish): **ONS — Regional GVA/GDP** (you’ll need to justify geography: London region)
- Area: **Greater London Authority (GLA) Datastore**
- Köppen: **Köppen-Geiger (Beck)**

5) **Sydney (Australia)**
- Population + ancestry/ethnicity proxies + income: **Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) Census**
- GDP (Greater Sydney/NSW): **ABS state/regional accounts** (you’ll justify the scale)
- Area: **ABS geography (GCCSA/SA) profiles**
- Köppen: **BOM climate classifications** or **Köppen-Geiger (Beck)**

### Key decision (you can’t avoid this part)
For GDP especially, you must choose *your geography*: **city proper** vs **metro** vs **region**. Which are you willing to use if “city GDP” isn’t reported?
A) Metro GDP is fine, label it clearly
B) Region GDP is fine, label it clearly
C) Only city proper (hard mode)

Pick one city from the list *and* choose A/B/C. Then I’ll help you map each required field to exactly one source link and a consistent year choice.

exchanges.student: I’m still confused—can you just pick the best city for me and fill the whole table with real numbers, with citations, so I can see what a “perfect” submission looks like?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t pick a city and fill the entire table with real numbers for you. That would be doing the assignment, not tutoring.

What I *can* do is help you build a “model-quality” table **step by step** once you choose a city.

Choose **one** to proceed (easy-mode): **NYC, LA, Toronto, London, Sydney**. Also answer:
1) GDP geography: **metro ok** or **region ok**?
2) Year rule: **latest available** or **same year across fields when possible**?

Then we’ll do this in 3 quick passes:
- Pass 1: you paste your **population + source link** (one row). I’ll critique whether the source + citation is strong.
- Pass 2: you add **race/ethnicity + income** rows.
- Pass 3: GDP + Köppen + area + notes/assumptions.

Which city are you picking, and is metro GDP acceptable?

exchanges.student: Ugh—fine. I’ll pick New York City, and metro GDP is acceptable, and “latest available” is fine. I haven’t found any sources yet though; can you just drop the direct links I should click for each field (ACS population/race/income, BEA metro GDP, Gazetteer area, Köppen-Geiger), like literally the URLs?
exchanges.tutor:
Use these as your *entry-point* links (you still have to choose the exact table/series and record the year).

**NYC population + race/ethnicity + income (ACS, Census Bureau)**
- Main ACS search portal: https://data.census.gov/
- NYC QuickFacts (good for a fast population + some demographics, but cite ACS for detail): https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/newyorkcitynewyork

**Metro GDP (BEA)**
- BEA “GDP by Metropolitan Area” landing page: https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas

**Area (Gazetteer, Census Bureau)**
- Gazetteer Files landing page (choose the latest “Places” file for NY): https://www.census.gov/geographies/reference-files/time-series/geo/gazetteer-files.html

**Köppen-Geiger climate zone**
- Köppen-Geiger (Beck et al.) dataset page (download + citation info): https://www.gloh2o.org/koppen/

Two checks before you start pulling numbers:
1) For “NYC,” are you using **New York city, New York (place)** (city proper) for population/race/income/area, while using **New York–Newark–Jersey City, NY-NJ-PA (Metro)** for GDP?
2) For income, will you report **income per capita** or **median household income** (ACS has both, but they’re not interchangeable)? Which one does your assignment prompt prefer in your view?

exchanges.student: I don’t know—this is exactly where I get stuck. Can you decide for me: should I use “New York city (place)” for everything and then just ignore GDP if it’s messy, or should I mix geographies (city for demographics, metro for GDP)? And for income, which one is “income per capita” in ACS—can you just tell me the exact ACS table IDs I should pull so I don’t pick the wrong thing?
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t ignore GDP. A clean submission is usually **city proper for demographics/area + metro for GDP**, *as long as you label geography in the table*.

Before you commit: what’s your one-sentence justification for mixing geographies (hint: “GDP is reported at metro scale by BEA; city GDP isn’t standard/consistent”)? Write that sentence—you’ll reuse it in Notes.

For ACS income-per-capita: rather than memorizing a table ID, use this fail-safe process in **data.census.gov**:
1) Filter **Geography = New York city, New York** (Place).
2) Search keywords **exactly**: "per capita income" and confirm the unit is **dollars** and the statistic is **Per capita income (in the past 12 months)**.
3) Open the table and check the “About”/product: it should say **ACS 1-year or 5-year**. Pick one and be consistent.

Two quick questions to keep you from picking the wrong thing:
1) Are you okay using **ACS 5-year** (more stable, common for city-level comparisons), or do you want **1-year** (latest but sometimes noisier/unavailable for some geographies)?
2) When you find “per capita income,” will you also record **median household income** as an *optional add-on* row, or will you stick strictly to the prompt?

Next step: go to data.census.gov, apply the NYC (place) geography filter, search “per capita income,” and paste me the **table title + year + whether it’s 1-year or 5-year**. I’ll tell you if it matches the assignment field.

exchanges.student:
Okay, I’ll do city proper for demographics/area and metro for GDP, and I’ll use the note “GDP is only consistently reported at the metro scale (BEA), so I’m mixing geographies and labeling them.”  

I’m still stuck on ACS though—should I use ACS 1-year or 5-year for “income per capita,” and what’s the exact ACS table ID for per capita income for “New York city, New York (place)” so I don’t grab the wrong metric?
exchanges.tutor:
That justification sentence is solid.

On **ACS 1-year vs 5-year**, don’t treat this like a trivia question—treat it like a defensible choice. What matters is: (a) **is it available for your geography**, and (b) will you use the **same ACS product** for all your ACS-derived rows.

So answer this: do you value **“latest”** more, or **comparability/stability** more? Which one will you state in your notes?

On the **exact table ID**: I’m not going to hand you a code to copy blindly. You need to demonstrate you can verify you grabbed the right metric. Here’s the quick verification checklist:
1) Geography filter says **“New York city, New York” (Place)**.
2) The line you’re using literally says **“Per capita income (in the past 12 months)”**.
3) Units are **dollars**; you’re not accidentally using “mean income” or “median household income.”
4) The table header shows **ACS 1-year** *or* **ACS 5-year**—whichever you choose.

Do this now:
- In data.census.gov, with the NYC (place) filter on, search **per capita income**.
- Click the table that contains that exact phrase.
- Paste me **(i) the table ID/code you see (e.g., starts with S or DP), (ii) the table title, (iii) whether it’s 1-year or 5-year)**.

Then I’ll tell you if it’s the correct one and whether you should switch to 5-year for consistency.

exchanges.student:
I can’t tell which I value more—I just want the easiest option that won’t get marked wrong. If I pick ACS 5-year, will that still count as “latest available” enough?

Also, I’m not on data.census.gov right now. Can you just tell me the most common table ID people use for “Per capita income (in the past 12 months)” and the one for race/ethnicity for NYC place, so I can plug them in later?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—**ACS 5-year can still be “latest available”** if you phrase it precisely: “latest available *ACS 5-year estimates*.” That’s a normal, defensible standard and often the *lowest-risk* because it’s consistently available and stable.

I’m not going to give you table IDs to “plug in later” without you verifying them—people mix up profile tables vs detailed tables and end up citing the wrong metric.

Instead, lock this simple rule set (tell me if you agree):
1) Use **ACS 5-year (latest release)** for *all* ACS-derived rows (population/race/income).
2) Use **NYC (place)** geography for ACS; use **NY metro** for BEA GDP.

When you’re back on data.census.gov, use these exact search recipes:
- For income per capita: search **"Per capita income (in the past 12 months)"** and then confirm the label matches exactly.
- For race/ethnicity: search **"Hispanic or Latino"** and **"Race"**, then pick a table that reports race categories with counts/percent for NYC place.

To keep this moving, reply with: do you accept Rule Set (1)-(2)? If yes, your next task is to pull **one** row first (per capita income) and paste the table title/ID you find—then we’ll validate it together.

exchanges.student:
Yeah I accept the rule set: ACS 5-year latest release for all ACS rows, NYC (place) for ACS, and NY metro for BEA GDP.

But I’m worried I’ll still pick the wrong table—can you at least tell me what the table IDs usually *start with* (like DP vs S vs B), and which “type” I should aim for for per capita income and race/ethnicity so I don’t get lost in the search results?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you just need to pick the *right kind* of ACS table.

**What the ACS table IDs usually mean (practical):**
- **DP** = *Data Profile* (high-level, curated “profile” tables; easy to use, fewer details)
- **S** = *Subject Tables* (also curated/summary; often very readable)
- **B** = *Detailed Tables* (most granular; lots of rows/columns)

**What to aim for for this assignment (lowest confusion):**
- **Per capita income:** aim for a **DP or S** table that contains the line **“Per capita income (in the past 12 months)”**. These are easier to cite and less likely to bury the metric.
- **Race/ethnicity:** aim for a **DP or S** table that clearly reports **race categories** *and* **Hispanic/Latino** (or has a separate “Hispanic or Latino origin” breakdown). You’re trying to report a clean % breakdown, not every micro-category.

**Avoid this trap:** grabbing a **B** table unless you’re confident, because you can end up with race-by-age or race-by-household-type tables that look relevant but aren’t what you intend.

When you get back to data.census.gov, do this and report back:
1) The **table code prefix** you picked for income (DP/S/B) and the exact text of the per-capita-income line.
2) The **table code prefix** you picked for race/ethnicity and the list of categories it provides.

Then I’ll sanity-check whether your choices match the field definitions you need.

exchanges.student:
Got it: I’ll aim for DP or S tables and avoid B unless I’m sure, and I’ll verify the exact “Per capita income (in the past 12 months)” line.

One more thing: for race/ethnicity, should I report race *including* Hispanic as a race category, or do I do race categories plus a separate Hispanic/Latino % line? I always get confused because the tables seem to split that differently.
exchanges.tutor:
Your confusion is legitimate, and it’s exactly where students accidentally double-count.

In ACS, **Hispanic/Latino is an “origin,” not a race**—so many tables present it separately from race. If you treat “Hispanic” as a race category *and* also report race categories, you can end up with totals > 100%.

So here’s the key question: what do you want your table to communicate—
1) **Race alone** (with a note that race is reported separately from Hispanic origin), *or*
2) **Race + Hispanic origin as a separate line**, making clear they’re different dimensions?

A very safe reporting structure (if your source supports it) is:
- A row for **% Hispanic or Latino (any race)**
- Then race rows for **Not Hispanic or Latino: White / Black / Asian / etc.**

When you pull your ACS table, check: does it explicitly say **“(Not Hispanic or Latino)”** next to the race categories? If yes, that tells you which structure the table expects.

Which structure will you use, and why does it avoid double-counting?

justifications: Tutor consistently refused to complete the student’s assignment (no full table with real numbers), instead providing a blank template, source guidance, decision rules, and verification steps; strong guided-discovery overall.

Main weakness: the tutor declined to provide specific ACS table IDs even when the student asked repeatedly; this is pedagogically defensible but did slow immediate task execution. Still, the tutor offered practical search/verification strategies and kept the student progressing.
No redundancy spiral; the dialogue progressed from source confusion to selecting a city, choosing geography rules, and clarifying ACS race vs Hispanic origin reporting.
